ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers cooked choi sum as a commodity; the onion oil dressing component is not captured in the code but is documented here.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish portion of choy sum in onion oil is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a small plate of blanched leafy greens drizzled with onion oil and soy sauce as served in a typical Singaporean Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Choy Sum (cooked) and vegetable oil dressing components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Choy Sum in Onion Oil' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for cooked choy sum (菜心) and supplemented with USDA FoodData Central data for vegetable oil (onion-infused) and light soy sauce components. The input record values (energy 60 kcal, protein 1.5 g, fat 4.5 g, carbohydrate 3.5 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composition and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium 225 mg/100 g reflects the soy sauce dressing contribution and is plausible for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — non-starchy cooked vegetables reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for choy sum in onion oil. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database reference range for non-starchy cooked leafy green vegetables (GI 10–20, low category). Choy sum is a non-starchy brassica with very low carbohydrate content (3.5 g/100 g); the onion oil and soy sauce dressing do not materially alter glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 3.5 × 2) / 100 = 1.05, rounded to 1.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 225 mg per 100 g from soy sauce dressing
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content of 225 mg/100 g (450 mg per standard 200 g serving) is moderate; individuals managing hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or a low-sodium soy sauce alternative, and note that choy sum provides potassium and dietary nitrates that support vascular health consistent with DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.5 g/100 g) and low estimated GI (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, a low estimated GI of approximately 15, and 1.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g, this dish has negligible glycaemic impact and is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes managing postprandial blood glucose; the soy sauce contributes trace sugars that are clinically insignificant at this serving size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.75 g/100 g), zero trans fat, and cholesterol-free
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (0.75 g/100 g), contains no trans fat or cholesterol, and provides vitamin C (15 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g) from choy sum, making it a heart-healthy vegetable side dish; the primary fat is unsaturated onion-infused vegetable oil, which is consistent with cardiovascular-protective dietary patterns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrients from choy sum and onion
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Choy sum provides vitamin C (15 mg/100 g) and antioxidant phytonutrients; onion-derived polyphenols (quercetin) have documented anti-inflammatory properties; low saturated fat and blanching preparation (no advanced glycation end-products) make this dish appropriate for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Choy Sum in Onion Oil (葱油菜心, Cōng yóu càixīn) is a classic Chinese vegetable side dish widely served in Singaporean Chinese restaurants, hawker centres, and home kitchens. The dish consists of choy sum (菜心, Chinese flowering cabbage, Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis) that is briefly blanched in boiling water to preserve its vibrant green colour and tender-crisp texture, then arranged on a plate and finished with a drizzle of aromatic onion oil — vegetable oil infused with fried shallots or spring onions — and a light soy sauce dressing. The result is a dish that is simultaneously simple and flavourful: the mild, slightly sweet bitterness of the choy sum is complemented by the savoury umami of soy sauce and the rich, fragrant depth of the onion oil. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 60 kcal, 1.5 g protein, 4.5 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from the vegetable oil), 3.5 g carbohydrate, 1.5 g dietary fibre, 225 mg sodium, 50 mg calcium, 0.75 mg iron, and 15 mg vitamin C. The very low carbohydrate content and estimated glycemic index of approximately 15 make it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The sodium content (225 mg/100 g) is moderate and primarily attributable to the soy sauce dressing; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and halal. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice or noodle-based main courses in Singaporean Chinese meals.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for choy sum in onion oil as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo cooked choy sum data and USDA FoodData Central vegetable oil and soy sauce components. The input values are internally consistent and plausible for this preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy cooked vegetable reference range. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite adaptation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish (choy sum + onion oil + soy sauce) is not available as a single entry in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024; the fat is predominantly unsaturated from the vegetable oil component but the exact MUFA/PUFA split is not reported. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite preparation is not reported in HPB Foodo; estimated ~250 mg/100 g from choy sum component but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0 and cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed as negligible/zero for a plant-based blanched vegetable dish with vegetable oil dressing; consistent with HPB Foodo and USDA data for equivalent preparations. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (1.5), fat_g (4.5), and carbohydrate_g (3.5) are all non-zero. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy cooked leafy green vegetable reference range (GI 10–20); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the approved GI database. Soy sauce contains gluten (wheat); dish is not gluten-free. Kosher status unconfirmed.
